summary: The post reflects on how people often grow more conservative with age, shifting from ambitious building to defensive protectionism. That mindset amplifies негативivity, fixates on past achievements, and overlooks future opportunity. Against this, the author argues that history shows progress and new ideas tend to win out over time, even if individual ideas fail. He frames the present as a choice between courageous exploration and clinging to an increasingly untenable status quo, warning that refusal to explore carries its own risks. The piece concludes that today’s regressive politics will likely be seen as short-sighted by future generations.
key_points: Aging social circles can drift toward protectionism, which magnifies negative thinking and narrows horizons.
Historical patterns suggest progress and fresh ideas prevail despite many failures.
Staying at a “local maximum” is unsafe; moving forward can reveal better vantage points.
Current political rhetoric leans regressive, stoking fear, individualism, and nationalism.
Future generations will likely judge today’s backward-looking impulses as short-sighted.
money_quotes: As I'm getting older a lot of my social circles are becoming ever more conservative.
The focus shifts from building with ambition to fiercly protecting what one has achieved.
Yet, when we look back at history, it becomes clear that progress and fresh ideas tend to prevail over time.
I believe that every day presents us with a choice: to step forward with courage and optimism or to cling to the status quo, even as it becomes increasingly untenable.
Embracing new ideas carries inherent risks, but so does the refusal to explore them.
Right now we find ourselves slowly sliding down from our local maximum and some people try to pull you back up to where we were standing.
But as time marches on, future generations will likely look back at these regressive inclinations and wonder how we could have been so short-sighted.
key_insight: Progress demands the courage to move beyond a fading local peak, because clinging to the past is as risky as innovation itself.
